study_area: Charlotte, NC -- Harris Teeter Site Selection
hardware: HPC gal-i9 cluster | Red Hat Enterprise Linux | Python 3.12.3

paper1_standard_dijkstra.description: 2450 individual scipy Dijkstra calls, one per candidate, full graph each time

paper2_parallel_dijkstra.description: 2450 Dijkstra calls distributed across 4 CPU cores via joblib

our_method_multisource_dijkstra.description: Single scipy Dijkstra call with all 559 BG centroids as simultaneous sources. Builds [559 x 282751] distance matrix once, then scoring is column lookups.
our_method_multisource_dijkstra.preprocessing_note: Build bg_dist_matrix [559 x 282751] -- saved to cache after first run

paper3_python_ch.description: CH contracts 282751 nodes by rank, adds shortcuts. Query phase uses bidirectional Dijkstra upward through contracted graph. NOTE: Horton et al. primary contribution is EDE facility location optimization -- CH/OSRM is only their routing backend.
paper3_python_ch.routing_mode: walking (OSRM); our benchmark uses driving (35 mph) for consistency
paper3_python_ch.preprocessing_note: Interrupted at 95% after 533s. Extrapolated total: ~1800-2400s (30-40 min). Pure Python implementation -- OSRM C++ backend would be ~5 min.
paper3_python_ch.query_scoring_note: Not measured (CH preprocessing not completed). Literature: ~3-5 min for 559 queries.
paper3_python_ch.osrm_cpp_note: OSRM C++ preprocessing: ~5 min (literature value, Horton et al. 2025)

key_finding: Multi-source Dijkstra (our method) achieves 4.7x speedup over standard sequential Dijkstra and 1.6x over parallel Dijkstra, while preprocessing completes in 44.3s vs 30-40 min for Python CH. After preprocessing, subsequent scoring runs take only 0.46s via cached distance matrix lookups.
